unit.name: Language modeling as pretraining and why fine-tuning works
unit.description: Defines language-model pretraining, explains the two-stage pretrain–fine-tune workflow, and discusses data scale, evaluation, and why pretrained initializations help.
unit.summary: Here pretraining is reduced to language modeling: train a model to predict the next token from the prefix, keep all learned weights, and reuse them as the initialization for a downstream task model [ts=1487s] [ts=1538s]. The lecture formalizes the now-standard two-step workflow: first learn broad linguistic regularities from abundant unlabeled text, then adapt the model to a smaller supervised task such as sentiment analysis with additional gradient updates [ts=1552s] [ts=1580s]. A major reason this helps is scale: unlabeled text is orders of magnitude more plentiful than labeled task data, especially in English, so pretraining can absorb generic knowledge about language and the world before fine-tuning specializes it [ts=1649s] [ts=1997s]. During development, perplexity is a practical proxy metric for pretraining quality, while benchmark suites serve as rough task-level checks of generality [ts=1758s] [ts=1774s]. The instructor also emphasizes that optimization matters: starting gradient descent from pretrained parameters often leads fine-tuning to better valleys than random initialization, even though the full theory remains open [ts=1812s] [ts=1875s].

key_points.text: Language-model pretraining predicts the next word from previous words and then reuses all learned parameters.
The standard workflow is pretraining on unlabeled text followed by fine-tuning on a smaller labeled task.
Unlabeled text is vastly more abundant than labeled task data, which is a core reason pretraining helps.
Perplexity is a convenient training-time metric that often correlates with downstream performance.
Fine-tuning works partly because optimization stays near strong pretrained parameters rather than starting from random weights.

A major reason this helps is scale: unlabeled text is orders of magnitude more plentiful than labeled task data, especially in English, so pretraining can absorb generic knowledge about language and the world before fine-tuning specializes it [ts=1649s] [ts=1997s]. kp_catalog.name: Pretraining, fine-tuning, and transfer learning
kp_catalog.description: Two-stage learning in which a model first learns generic language structure from large unlabeled corpora and is then adapted to a supervised downstream task. Reusing pretrained CNN features or pretrained weights to solve a new task more effectively when target data is limited.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because pretraining, fine-tuning, and transfer learning formalizes how two-stage learning in which a model first learns generic language structure from large unlabeled corpora and is then adapted to a supervised downstream task, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context though roles split between capstone and gateway.

kp_catalog.name: Autoregressive language modeling
kp_catalog.description: Training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly. Autoregressive models make sequence modeling tractable by factoring p(x) into conditional next-part distributions over an ordered decomposition of x.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because autoregressive language modeling formalizes how training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.
